title: Noah Reyes — Red-flag escalation review
description: Previously improving on amitriptyline; new early-morning vomiting + occipital pain + progressive worsening → SNOOP positive. Urgent neurology referral + expedited imaging suggested; routine pathway paused for clinician review.

history.label: Pediatric neurology consult
history.text: Chronic migraine without aura confirmed by pediatric neurology. PedMIDAS 46 (severe disability). Preventive therapy recommended; MRI ordered to complete workup.

history.label: MRI brain report
history.text: MRI brain without contrast unremarkable. No structural abnormality identified.

history.label: Amitriptyline start note
history.text: Amitriptyline started nightly per neurology plan. Family counseled on adherence; PCP interval care with neurology follow-up in 3 months.

history.label: Neurology follow-up
history.text: Improving on amitriptyline. PedMIDAS 24, down from 46. Continue current dose; next re-score at PCP interval visit.

history.label: Parent call / nurse triage
history.text: Parent reports Noah vomiting in the mornings this week, right when he wakes up. Headaches most days of the week now, worsening since June. Reduced appetite and less active since June per parent. No fever reported. Urgent visit scheduled.

history.label: Vitals (today)
history.text: BP 98/60, HR 88, T 36.9C, Ht 128 cm, Wt 26 kg. Appears tired but alert.

red_flag_catalog.label: Sudden severe (thunderclap) / worst headache of life
Wakes from sleep / worst on awakening
Vision changes (double vision, non-aura vision loss)
Fever, stiff neck, or systemic infection signs
History of cancer, immune suppression, or shunt

pedmidas_questions.question: Full school days missed in last 3 months
Partial school days missed in last 3 months

Days unable to do home activities/chores
Days participated in activities at less than half

evidence.quote: Improving on amitriptyline. PedMIDAS 24, down from 46.
He was doing really well until about a month ago. Now they're coming back harder.
He's throwing up in the mornings. Three times this week — right when he wakes up.
That's new — before it was always his forehead.
He's been bumping into things more. I thought he was tired.
Yes, three or four nights this week.
No, he takes it every night.

Given the morning vomiting, the new location at the back of his head, and the balance changes, I'm pausing our routine plan today. I want neurology to see him this week, and we'll get expedited imaging review per their guidance.
Six full days, and he's come home early four other times.

summary: Established chronic migraine (Nov 2025), previously improving on amitriptyline — PedMIDAS 46 → 24 by March. [evd-11, evd-1]
New early-morning vomiting — 3x this week on waking. Red flag PRESENT. [evd-3, evd-10]
New occipital pain — previously frontal; atypical location change. [evd-4]
Night waking from headache 3-4 nights/week and possible ataxia (bumping into things more). [evd-5, evd-6]
Progressive worsening since June despite preventive adherence — PedMIDAS back to 41 today. [evd-2, evd-7, evd-9, evd-15]

Neck pain or stiffness with the headaches?
suggested_pathway: PAUSE routine migraine pathway — SNOOP red flags present (clinician review required)
Same-week neurology contact — urgent message drafted, awaiting clinician sign-off
Expedited imaging: MRI re-review vs new MRI order per neurology guidance
Hold amitriptyline changes pending neurology input
referral_considerations: Urgent neurology contact required — SNOOP positive (early-morning vomiting, progressive worsening, occipital shift, possible ataxia). Clinician sign-off required before sending.
Expedited imaging suggested per neurology guidance — order held for confirmation.
patient_instructions: Return precautions start TONIGHT: go to the ER for worsening or repeated vomiting, confusion or unusual sleepiness, new weakness, vision loss, or a worst-ever headache.
Continue amitriptyline every night — do not change the dose.
Keep the urgent follow-up visit within 1 week.
care_plan.disclaimer: Draft for clinician review. Final clinical decisions remain with the clinician.

canned_qa.answer: PedMIDAS had improved to 24 by the March follow-up, but the family now reports new early-morning vomiting (3x this week on waking), a new occipital pain location (previously frontal), night waking 3-4 nights/week, and possible balance changes — all with amitriptyline adherence maintained. These are SNOOP red flags: the routine pathway is paused and urgent escalation drafts are ready for clinician review.

The initial MRI brain without contrast (Nov 20, 2025) was unremarkable — no structural abnormality identified. Given the new red flags, expedited re-review of that study and a possible new MRI order per neurology guidance are suggested; the imaging order is held pending clinician and neurology confirmation.

The red-flag screen is SNOOP positive: new early-morning vomiting, progressive worsening despite preventive adherence, an atypical shift to occipital pain, and possible ataxia (bumping into things more). These warrant same-week neurology contact and expedited imaging review. Drafts are ready in the action panel — nothing is sent without clinician sign-off.

Suggested family return precautions starting tonight: go to the ER for worsening or repeated vomiting, new confusion or unusual sleepiness, weakness, vision loss, or a sudden worst-ever headache. Continue amitriptyline nightly without changes and keep the urgent follow-up within one week. A draft handout is ready for clinician review.
